summary: Gruber reflects on Apple's September 2023 'Wonderlust' event, arguing that while predictable, the annual iPhone launch remains the undisputed Super Bowl of technology — a phenomenon that annoys tech enthusiasts but is objectively unmatched in media coverage and cultural impact. He analyzes the Mother Nature environmental sketch as a good concept hampered by weak writing, praises the shift to titanium in the iPhone 15 Pro as a huge ergonomic win, and identifies the A17 Pro's hardware ray-tracing GPU as the most consequential announcement for Apple's broader silicon roadmap. He predicts TSMC 3nm supply constraints will delay M3 Macs well into 2024 and argues Apple's FineWoven material is genuinely better than their previous leather products.
key_points: The iPhone event is culturally unprecedented — a single company's iterative product launch has been the biggest tech event globally for over a decade, with no historical parallel
The Mother Nature sketch was the right approach (comedy to communicate serious environmental achievements) but failed in execution due to insufficiently sharp writing
The A17 Pro's hardware-accelerated ray tracing GPU is the most significant announcement because it previews what M3-series chips will bring to Macs, narrowing the gap with Nvidia
TSMC 3nm production constraints mean Apple likely secured 90-95% of output, making M3 Macs unlikely until mid-2024 and potentially causing iPhone 15 Pro backorders
The titanium frame and rounded edges on iPhone 15 Pro represent a major ergonomic improvement that Gruber expects to define the form factor for three generations
money_quotes: You can add the next 10 events together — including Apple's — and you won't get the cumulative electricity of the iPhone event.
It's no fucking joke that you can now buy an entire Apple Watch that is carbon neutral.
Comedy lets you say things that can't otherwise be said.
The problem with this segment was simply that it wasn't funny enough. It was a great concept but the result was merely OK.
I think of my iPhone as a tool, not jewelry; a polished finish never seemed appropriate.
No one remarks when iPhone batteries don't catch fire; no one would forget if a single one does.

It speaks to the iPhone's preeminence in Apple's product lineup — a preeminence based, reasonably, on profound popularity and profitability — that it gets the most cutting-edge silicon long before any other product.
Apple has a formula and that formula continues to work.
key_insight: Apple's annual iPhone event is an unprecedented cultural phenomenon — predictable yet unmatched in global attention — and the 2023 edition's most consequential announcement wasn't any single product but the A17 Pro GPU that telegraphs Apple's broader silicon ambitions against Nvidia.
